content:
Damp heat aging represents a coupled thermo-hygroscopic degradation pathway in which elevated temperature and humidity act synergistically to undermine the adhesion integrity of polymeric encapsulant systems. The underlying mechanism is driven by the dual effect of temperature on both the chemical reaction rate and the moisture diffusion coefficient: higher temperatures accelerate hydrolytic and oxidative reactions at the encapsulant–substrate interface while simultaneously increasing the rate at which water molecules penetrate the polymer bulk and accumulate at interfacial regions [REF:1]. This synergistic coupling distinguishes damp heat aging from purely thermal or purely humid exposure, as the combined condition promotes more extensive water diffusion into the adhesive matrix and further accelerates degradation of the bonded joint [REF:1].

The temperature dependence of interfacial adhesion loss generally follows Arrhenius-type kinetics, in which the rate of adhesion degradation scales exponentially with temperature. Even in the absence of ultraviolet radiation and elevated humidity, thermal degradation of the encapsulant–substrate interface can be significant [REF:2], indicating that temperature alone provides sufficient activation energy to disrupt interfacial bonding. Thermal aging by 5,000 thermocycles induced a significant decrease in micro-tensile bond strength values across all seven adhesive systems tested, with the magnitude of degradation varying by material type and one-step self-etch adhesives exhibiting the lowest residual bond strength [REF:3]. This material-dependent thermal susceptibility, observed even under dry thermal cycling conditions [REF:2][REF:3], underscores the importance of intrinsic polymer and interface chemistry in governing degradation kinetics.

The effects of damp heat aging on adhesive systems can be categorized into reversible and irreversible components. Reversible effects are primarily moisture-driven: water molecules that occupy free volume within the polymer matrix can be removed by drying, restoring a portion of the original mechanical properties. Irreversible effects, by contrast, arise from hydrolytic cleavage of chemical bonds at the interface—particularly the silane coupling agent bonds between encapsulant and glass—as well as the formation of microcracks and permanent structural defects within the polymer network. The irreversible pathway is accelerated under damp heat conditions because the higher diffusion coefficient at elevated temperature [REF:1] delivers more water to the reactive interface per unit time, increasing the cumulative hydrolytic damage.

A critical gap in the current evidence base is the scarcity of data specific to DVS-PEI (divinylsiloxane-bisphenol A dianhydride polyetherimide) encapsulants in flexible electronics. The available mechanistic framework—derived primarily from EVA and adhesive resin systems—establishes that thermal and moisture stressors interact non-linearly to degrade interfacial adhesion [REF:1][REF:2], but the transferability of these findings to DVS-PEI chemistry, which features distinct backbone stiffness and moisture uptake characteristics, remains unverified.

**Key Findings**

- Damp heat aging degrades adhesive joints through synergistic coupling of accelerated chemical reaction rates and increased moisture diffusion at elevated temperatures [REF:1].
- Thermal degradation of the encapsulant–substrate interface can be significant even without UV or elevated humidity, demonstrating that temperature alone activates interfacial bond disruption [REF:2].
- Thermal cycling induced significant micro-tensile bond strength decreases across all adhesive systems tested, with material-dependent degradation severity [REF:3].
- The distinction between reversible (moisture-driven plasticization) and irreversible (hydrolytic bond cleavage, microcracks) degradation mechanisms is critical for predicting long-term adhesion retention [REF:1][REF:2].
- DVS-PEI–specific damp heat aging data remains a significant gap requiring targeted experimental investigation.
